intro_en: Voice, graphical interfaces, and robotics converge on two requirements: continuous multimodal perception and action within a strict latency budget. This section traces the transition from sequential pipelines to streaming, fast–slow separation, and full-duplex or hierarchical architectures.

concepts.explanation_en: The author defines three paradigms. A Cascaded Voice Pipeline passes control through VAD → ASR → LLM → TTS; Full-Chain Streaming preserves modularity while overlapping recognition, generation, and synthesis in time. An End-to-End Omnimodal Model unifies listening, reasoning, and speech in one model and better preserves prosody, emotion, and background sound, but it usually still depends on detecting the end of an utterance. A Full-Duplex / Interactive Model abandons turn-taking itself: input and output audio streams are processed concurrently, so overlapping speech and interruption do not require a separate barge-in pipeline. Full-chain streaming reduces latency to roughly 600–800 ms but does not eliminate VAD’s silence wait. Extrapolation (not a claim made by the book): A production voice architecture should be selected according to its measured first-audio latency budget, the cost of false interruptions, and the value of paralinguistic information.
concepts.analogy_en: A cascade resembles a relay race, a streaming cascade resembles assembly-line stations working concurrently, and full duplex resembles two people who can listen and speak with overlap instead of waiting for a formal end of turn.
concepts.visual_title_en: Visual Model: Cascaded, End-to-End Omnimodal, and Full-Duplex Voice Architectures

concepts.explanation_en: Real-time interaction conflicts with deep reasoning. In Solution 1, fast and slow answers are generated independently: this produces an early response but causes redundant computation and contradictions. In Solution 2, the slow model becomes a background strategist while the fast model sustains the conversation; their textual interface is implemented through the Agent Status Bar. Solution 3, Step-Audio R1, internalizes reasoning and speech in one system: Modality-Grounded Reasoning Distillation selects reasoning grounded in acoustic features, while Mind-Paced Speaking divides work between a Formulation Brain and an Articulation Brain operating as a pipeline. In Experiment 9-4 on Spoken-MQA, direct answering scored 70.6%, MPS Speak-First 92.8%, MPS Think-First 93.9%, and full Think-Before-Speak 93.0%. Extrapolation (not a claim made by the book): A background strategist needs a versioned state contract with confidence, freshness, pending tools, and supersedes fields, or the fast model may voice stale advice. A latent channel is justified only after an ablation against a text protocol: it is faster and richer, but less observable and harder to debug.
concepts.analogy_en: A textual Agent Status Bar is like notes passed between two people solving a problem: the conclusion is conveyed, but the reasoning draft remains hidden. A Latent Bridge attempts to transmit a compact internal representation of that draft rather than a note.
concepts.visual_title_en: Visual Model: Fast–Slow Thinking Architectures and the Latent Bridge

concepts.explanation_en: Computer Use operates in a Perceive–Think–Act loop: capture a screenshot, run model inference, act with the mouse or keyboard, wait for the interface to stabilize, and capture another screenshot. Visual Grounding is responsible for locating the target. Set-of-Mark segments the image and numbers regions; DOM/Accessibility Tree Indexing extracts interactive elements from the interface structure and turns the task into ID selection; Pure Coordinate Prediction outputs coordinates directly and requires consistent scaling between the actual and training resolutions. Static screenshots miss video, short-lived notifications, and sound. For the web and accessible native applications, the book recommends the DOM/Accessibility Tree as the most accurate and stable route; for Canvas/WebGL, games, and nonstandard controls, visual annotation and coordinate prediction remain necessary. Extrapolation (not a claim made by the book): A production observer can collect a unified log of DOM changes, keyframes, transcripts, and accessibility events with shared timestamps. The perception policy should be adapted to the model and task instead of keeping every channel active at all times.
concepts.analogy_en: Set-of-Mark and DOM indexing turn coordinate search from an open-ended question into a multiple-choice test: the agent selects [123], and the executor already knows the element’s geometry.
concepts.visual_title_en: Visual Model: Visual Grounding and the Agent–Computer Observation Interface

concepts.explanation_en: The main unresolved limit of Computer Use is not only accuracy but also the efficiency of the sequential screenshot–think–click loop. Later steps slow down as context grows, and the agent cannot reliably plan its next action while executing the current one. The workaround described does not accelerate the GUI loop itself: a fast voice model keeps the user engaged while a slow VLM operates the browser. They exchange a minimal plain-text contract—a rolling status containing completed work, required information, and a completion indicator. This is not a visual-grounding error but a deployment-environment constraint. In Experiment 9-6, each Anthropic Computer Use action took 2–5 seconds. In Experiment 9-7, browser-use completed a five-step search in about 20 seconds. Extrapolation (not a claim made by the book): The status contract should prevent the fast model from declaring an irreversible outcome without a completion flag from the execution loop. For login, purchase, or form submission, separate planned, executing, observed, and verified states are more useful than a single conversational “done.”
concepts.analogy_en: The user talks to a fast operator while a slower executor works at the computer; a short status line serves as a shared board that prevents either participant from presenting an intention as a completed action.
concepts.visual_title_en: Visual Model: Fast–Slow Decoupling for Real-Time Computer Use

concepts.explanation_en: A robot makes decisions at two time scales. A slow Long-Horizon Planner decomposes the goal and selects subgoals; a fast Vision-Language-Action Controller continuously transforms images and instructions into control signals. This separation answers “what to do” and “how to do it” and is distinct from MPS’s separation of reasoning and speech articulation. Action Chunking compensates for VLA inference running at roughly 1–10 Hz while conventional control requires 50–1,000 Hz. Instead of producing one action, the model generates a short future trajectory; the control thread replays it at high frequency while the next chunk is computed in the background. The original single-step OpenVLA setup runs at about 6 Hz. Extrapolation (not a claim made by the book): A chunk should be interruptible through watchdog signals such as a person entering the scene, divergence between expected and observed pose, rising force, or falling confidence. Chunk length can shrink dynamically near obstacles and grow along predictable open segments.
concepts.analogy_en: Action Chunking resembles video buffering: the model loads a short segment of motion in advance so the executor can play it without pauses, but an overly large buffer delays the response to a sudden scene change.
concepts.visual_title_en: Visual Model: Planning–Control Hierarchy and Action Chunking

concepts.explanation_en: VLA controllers are trained primarily through imitation of human demonstrations. RT-2 and OpenVLA represent continuous actions as discrete action tokens and generate them autoregressively; π₀ uses flow matching to produce a continuous trajectory. Open X-Embodiment combines demonstrations from more than 20 platforms to learn shared grasping and placement patterns, after which the model is adapted with a small dataset from a specific robot. Sim2Real Transfer addresses a different gap: simulation does not reproduce physics, visuals, and actuator dynamics precisely. Domain Randomization varies friction, mass, lighting, textures, camera position, latency, and noise so that the real world falls within the training distribution. Experiment 9-10 uses LeRobot + ManiSkill, RGB input only, PPO, and 1,024 parallel environments. Extrapolation (not a claim made by the book): Transfer should be accepted against a matrix of shifts—camera, light, object, surface, actuator latency, and specific platform—not a single average success rate. Failing cells should expand the randomization range or guide the collection of real demonstrations rather than being hidden by an aggregate metric.
concepts.analogy_en: Domain Randomization makes the training world diverse enough that reality becomes another sample from a familiar range; calibration keeps that range from becoming either too narrow or meaninglessly broad.
concepts.visual_title_en: Visual Model: Cross-Embodiment Generalization and Sim2Real Transfer
